food_subgroup: Steamed then fried fish sausage (sago-starch bound)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Keropok Lekor. Closest parent is fish-starch sausage/cake fried snack. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and sago starch sausage, steamed then fried (Malay keropok lekor style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0FRP is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for processed fish-starch products. No exact Keropok Lekor code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Keropok Lekor or equivalent Malaysian fish sausage snack.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Keropok Lekor is approximately 5 fried pieces, weighing around 100g, commonly eaten as a snack at hawker stalls or street food markets.

nutrient_source.title: Keropok Lekor (fried) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD database entry for Keropok Lekor (fried). This is the primary approved regional source for Malaysian foods. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sago and Tapioca Starch Products — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Keropok Lekor in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the sago/tapioca starch component (GI ~70 for pure sago starch) moderated downward by the high fish protein (10.5g/100g) and fat (10.8g/100g) content, which significantly blunt the glycaemic response. This estimation methodology is consistent with published mixed-food GI moderation principles. GL per 100g serving = (65 × 17.5) / 100 = 11.4.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Keropok Lekor intake, as a single 100g serving provides 620mg sodium — approximately 27% of the recommended daily limit of 2,300mg — and the traditional sweet chilli dipping sauce adds further sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load (GL 11.4 per 100g) from sago/tapioca starch, partially mitigated by fish protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Keropok Lekor in moderate portions, as the GL of 11.4 per 100g serving is moderate and the high fish protein content (10.5g/100g) helps blunt the glycaemic response of the sago starch binder; portion control and avoidance of high-sugar dipping sauces are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and deep-frying fat absorption, partially offset by fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids and selenium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Keropok Lekor cautiously due to its high sodium content (620mg/100g) and fat from deep-frying, though the fish base provides cardioprotective marine omega-3 fatty acids and selenium (18mcg/100g), which support vascular and antioxidant health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids and selenium support joint and antioxidant health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Keropok Lekor may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the fish base provides marine omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties, and selenium (18mcg/100g, approximately 33% of the RDA) acts as an important antioxidant for joint health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keropok Lekor is a beloved and iconic East Coast Malaysian street food specialty originating from the coastal state of Terengganu. It is made from a high-fish-content paste of minced round scad (ikan tamban) or Indian mackerel (ikan kembung) blended with sago or tapioca starch, salt, and a small amount of sugar. The mixture is shaped into thick, sausage-like logs, then steamed until fully cooked through. The steamed version ('Keropok Lekor Rebus') is soft, dense, and chewy with an intensely savory fish flavour. The more popular fried version ('Keropok Lekor Goreng') is sliced into rounds and deep-fried until the exterior becomes golden and crispy while the interior remains dense, chewy, and richly flavoured. Keropok Lekor has a distinctive greyish colour from the high fish content and is traditionally served piping hot with a sweet and spicy chilli dipping sauce. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-calorie snack (195 kcal/100g) with meaningful fish protein (10.5g/100g), moderate fat from deep-frying (10.8g/100g), and a moderate carbohydrate load from the starch binder (17.5g/100g). It is a notable source of vitamin B12 (1.5mcg/100g), selenium (18mcg/100g), calcium (55mg/100g), and niacin (3.2mg/100g). The high sodium content (620mg/100g) is a key clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Keropok Lekor is a prized hawker snack sold at Terengganu food stalls, Ramadan bazaars, and East Coast Malaysian street food markets.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD database (nutriweb.org.my), which is the primary approved regional source for Malaysian foods under Project Veritas source policy. The food entry is an exact match for Keropok Lekor (fried). Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are supplemented from enriched_json at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate based on sago/tapioca starch GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by fish protein and fat content.
notes: All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a fish-starch fried snack. No zero-macro justification required. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate — no direct Keropok Lekor GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; the estimate is based on sago starch GI (~70) moderated by the high fish protein and fat content. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported in the IMR MyFCD source; set to -1. Water content not reported; set to -1. Copper (0.08mg), manganese (0.15mg), and vitamin K (1.0mcg) values are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 and should be verified against a primary source in a future review cycle. The food is halal and gluten-free. This is a Malaysian Malay dish; Singaporean multilingual requirements (Hindi) do not apply. Hindi local_name is null as this is not a Singaporean dish.
